food_subgroup: Rice vermicelli noodle soup — catfish broth thickened with chickpea flour and banana stem, topped with fish cake, split-pea fritters, and boiled egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Mohinga. Closest parent would be a generic noodle soup dish. This is the first systematic Myanmar cuisine entry in this database. FoodOn gap documented; null is appropriate.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle soup — closest parent used for Myanmar catfish rice vermicelli soup (Mohinga); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CMF is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a noodle-based soup dish. No exact code for Mohinga exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Mohinga or Myanmar noodle soup dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Mohinga weighs approximately 500g, comprising roughly 280g broth, 120g rice vermicelli noodles, and 100g toppings (split-pea fritters, fish cake, and boiled egg), providing approximately 410 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (2020) — component calculation for Mohinga using catfish, rice vermicelli, chickpea flour, banana stem, egg, and fish cake sub-entries; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for individual ingredient anchors
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Mohinga as a composite dish. Values are derived by component calculation: rice vermicelli (USDA FoodData Central #20066), catfish (USDA FoodData Central #15011), chickpea flour (USDA FoodData Central #16056), boiled egg (USDA FoodData Central #01129), split-pea fritters estimated from chana dal + frying oil, banana stem from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 banana blossom/stem entry. Composite weighted by portion mass (280g broth + 120g noodles + 100g toppings) and expressed per 100g of the assembled dish. Confidence: moderate (0.7).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice vermicelli and analogous noodle soup composite — University of Sydney GI Database; rice vermicelli GI 58–62 (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care); composite GI adjusted for chickpea flour thickener (GI ~39, University of Sydney GI Database) using FAO/WHO mixed-meal GI estimation method
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mohinga as a composite dish. GI ~60 is estimated from: (1) rice vermicelli GI 58–62 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008); (2) chickpea flour GI ~39 (University of Sydney GI Database); (3) composite GI weighted by carbohydrate contribution of each component per the FAO/WHO mixed-meal GI estimation method. The chickpea flour thickener (~15g chickpea flour per 500g bowl) provides a modest downward GI adjustment from the rice vermicelli baseline. GL = GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 60 × 52g / 100 = 31.2. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — approximately 950mg per 500g bowl (190mg per 100g), representing ~47.5% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2,000mg, primarily from fish sauce and shrimp paste in the broth
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume Mohinga with caution; leaving approximately half the broth reduces sodium intake by an estimated 300–400mg per serving, bringing the effective sodium load to a more manageable 500–650mg for a breakfast meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (~60) and high glycemic load (~31.2 per bowl) due to rice vermicelli base; partially mitigated by chickpea flour thickener (GI ~39), 18g protein from catfish and egg, and 14g fat from fritters providing glycaemic moderation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion and consume the full protein and fat components (catfish, egg) to moderate the postprandial glucose response; the chickpea flour broth provides a genuine but modest GI advantage over plain rice noodle soups.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (0.8g per 100g; ~4g per bowl) from fried split-pea fritters and fish cake; high sodium (~950mg per bowl) is the primary cardiovascular concern; catfish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids partially offsetting cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Mohinga to occasional consumption, leave most of the broth to reduce sodium, and request fritters to be minimised or omitted to lower saturated fat and fried-oil intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Catfish provides vitamin D (~1.5mcg per serving) and selenium; lemongrass contains citral with mild anti-inflammatory properties; split-pea fritters (fried oil) represent the primary pro-inflammatory element
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mohinga is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the catfish, egg, and lemongrass broth provide anti-inflammatory micronutrients, though patients should request reduced fritters to minimise fried-oil intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mohinga (မုန့်ဟင်းခါး) is Myanmar's national dish and most iconic breakfast food — a bowl of thin rice vermicelli noodles served in a rich, golden catfish broth thickened with toasted chickpea flour and the fibrous pith of young banana stem. The broth is built from freshwater catfish (nga-ku or similar Mekong catfish species) simmered with lemongrass, ginger, shallots, fish sauce, and shrimp paste, producing a deeply savoury, slightly earthy soup with a silky, lightly thickened consistency. Toppings include crispy split-pea fritters, sliced fish cake, a halved boiled egg, and sometimes sliced green banana or crispy fried garlic. The dish is eaten throughout Myanmar for breakfast and is available in Singapore at Golden Mile Complex (Beach Road) Burmese stalls from approximately 7am. Per standard bowl (~500g): approximately 410 kcal, 18g protein, 14g fat, 52g carbohydrate, GI ~60 (medium), GL ~31.2, and ~950mg sodium. The chickpea flour thickener (GI ~39) provides a modest glycaemic advantage over plain rice noodle soups. Nutritionally, Mohinga delivers meaningful protein from catfish and egg, selenium and vitamin D from catfish fat, and dietary fibre from banana stem and chickpea flour. The primary clinical concerns are high sodium from fermented condiments (fish sauce, shrimp paste) and a high glycaemic load per bowl. This is the first systematic Myanmar cuisine entry in this food database, derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 component calculation cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient anchors.

mohinga: မုန့်ဟင်းခါး

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Mohinga as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived by component calculation using FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient anchors, weighted by estimated portion mass. GI is estimated from rice vermicelli and chickpea flour GI values via the FAO/WHO mixed-meal method. Confidence is moderate (0.7) consistent with the input record.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for the Mohinga composite dish is not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level; individual catfish fatty acid data exist but cannot be reliably propagated to the assembled dish without introducing unacceptable estimation error. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content of the assembled dish (including broth) is not reported in the component sources at the composite level. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for this dish composition — catfish, rice vermicelli, chickpea flour, and egg do not contribute industrial trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component entries. 4. This is the first systematic Myanmar cuisine entry in this database. Nutrient values should be treated as moderate-confidence estimates pending direct laboratory analysis of Mohinga as prepared at representative Myanmar food stalls. 5. Halal status: Mohinga is traditionally halal (no pork); shrimp paste (ngapi) is used in some preparations — halal flag retained from input as the dish is widely served at halal-certified Burmese stalls in Singapore. Consumers with strict halal requirements should verify shrimp paste sourcing. 6. Gluten-free flag: rice vermicelli is gluten-free; chickpea flour is gluten-free; no wheat-based ingredients in the standard recipe. Flag retained. 7. GI source: analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice vermicelli and chickpea flour entries; no direct Mohinga GI measurement exists in the published literature as of April 2026.
